data.title: GDEP ADVANCEInc (TSE:5885) EPS Surge To ¥57.46 Tests Rich Valuation Narratives
data.description:
GDEP ADVANCEInc (TSE:5885) just posted its Q2 2026 numbers, reporting revenue of ¥2.1b and basic EPS of ¥57.46, supported by net income of ¥312.25m. The company’s quarterly revenue has moved from ¥1.19b in Q4 2025 and ¥948.85m in Q1 2026 to ¥2.1b in Q2 2026. Over the same period, basic EPS has ranged from ¥9.26 in Q3 2025 to ¥57.46 in the latest quarter, giving investors a results set where higher sales and earnings are working through to healthier margins.

See our full analysis for GDEP ADVANCEInc.

With the quarter reported, the next step is to compare these numbers with the prevailing market narratives to see which views hold up and which might need a rethink.

Margins Holding Up Year Over Year

  • Over the last 12 months, GDEP ADVANCEInc recorded an 11.2% net profit margin compared with 9.3% a year earlier, alongside trailing revenue of ¥5,718 million and net income of ¥640 million.
  • What stands out for the bullish view is that higher recent margins sit next to more moderate 5.6% earnings growth over the past year, compared with a 19.6% annualized pace over five years, which
    • supports the idea that profitability has held up, with margin improvement from 9.3% to 11.2% even as growth has been slower than the longer term trend,
    • but also shows that anyone leaning bullish on long run earnings compounding needs to weigh the latest 5.6% figure against that stronger multi year history.

Premium P/E Against Peers

  • The shares trade on a trailing P/E of 26.5x, compared with a peer average of 9.8x and a JP Electronic industry average of 15.4x, with the stock price at ¥3,120.
  • Critics highlight this premium as a bearish concern, and the numbers give that view some backing because
    • the current price of ¥3,120 sits well above the DCF fair value of ¥1,876.42, so valuation screens flag a gap between price and that model driven estimate,
    • while last year’s earnings growth rate of 5.6% is lower than the 19.6% five year annualized figure, which makes the higher than peer and industry P/E stand out even more.

Quarterly Profit Trend In Focus

  • Net income moved from ¥113.88 million in Q1 2025 to ¥135.75 million in Q1 2026 and ¥312.25 million in Q2 2026, alongside basic EPS figures of ¥21.27, ¥25.06 and ¥57.46 over those same quarters.
  • Consensus style thinking often wants multi period profit trends to align neatly, and the data here adds a mixed picture because
    • the trailing 12 month net income of ¥640 million and basic EPS of ¥86.34 JPY sit alongside that 5.6% recent earnings growth rate, showing profits have been solid in absolute terms even if growth has cooled versus the five year average,
    • yet the stock has also shown higher than market volatility over the past three months, which means these profit swings can be paired with sharper price reactions than a lower volatility name might see.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Gold Royalty (GROY) After 291% One Year Surge?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Gold Royalty's share price reflects its real worth, or if the recent excitement has run ahead of itself, this article explains what the numbers might indicate about value.
  • The stock most recently closed at US$4.85, with returns of 9.5% over 7 days, 19.2% over 30 days, 18.9% year to date and 291.1% over the past year, which has understandably caught the attention of many investors.
  • These moves sit against a backdrop of ongoing interest in royalty and streaming businesses tied to the gold sector, with investors often using them as a different way to gain exposure to the underlying commodity. That context helps explain why sentiment around Gold Royalty can shift quickly as views on gold related assets change.
  • Gold Royalty currently has a valuation score of 3 out of 6. Next, we will look at what different valuation approaches suggest about the stock, and then finish with a way of assessing value that can give you a broader perspective than any single method alone.

Gold Royalty delivered 291.1% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Metals and Mining industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Gold Royalty Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes projected future cash flows and discounts them back to today to estimate what the entire business might be worth in present value terms.

For Gold Royalty, the model used here is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of about US$37.6 million. Analysts provide estimates out to 2027, with free cash flow for that year projected at US$28.9 million, and Simply Wall St extrapolates further to arrive at a ten year path of cash flows.

Those projected cash flows, when discounted back to today, result in an estimated intrinsic value of about US$4.96 per share. Compared with the recent share price of US$4.85, the DCF suggests the stock trades at roughly a 2.3% discount, which is a very small gap and well within the sort of margin where reasonable assumptions can swing the outcome either way.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Gold Royalty is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Gold Royalty.

Approach 2: Gold Royalty Price vs Book

For companies where earnings can be uneven, investors often look at the Price to Book, or P/B, ratio, because it compares what the market is paying to the net assets backing the business. It is a simple way to check how much of a premium or discount you are paying to the company’s book value.

In general, higher growth expectations or a lower risk profile can support a higher P/B ratio. Slower growth or higher risk usually lines up with a lower P/B that sits closer to asset value. That context matters when you line Gold Royalty up against its sector.

Gold Royalty currently trades on a P/B of 2.13x. The Metals and Mining industry average is around 2.54x, while the peer group average provided here is 4.14x, so the stock sits below both of those reference points. Simply Wall St’s proprietary Fair Ratio framework goes a step further by estimating the P/B you might expect given factors such as earnings growth, industry, profit margin, market cap and risk. Because it ties the ratio to company specific fundamentals rather than simple comparisons, it can be more informative than just lining up against industry or peer averages. In this case, no Fair Ratio value is available, so you need to weigh the lower P/B against your own view of Gold Royalty’s assets, risks and prospects.

data.title: IG Port (TSE:3791) Margin Compression Challenges Bullish Growth Narratives
data.description:
IG Port (TSE:3791) has just posted Q2 2026 results with revenue of ¥3,147 million and basic EPS of ¥2.87, alongside trailing twelve month revenue of ¥13,119 million and EPS of ¥31.27 that sit against a reported net profit margin of 4.7%. Over the past six quarters, revenue has moved between ¥3,063 million and ¥4,479 million, while quarterly EPS has ranged from ¥2.87 to ¥12.65, giving investors a clear view of how earnings support has shifted around that margin. With earnings having grown at about 22.2% per year over five years but a weaker trailing margin and earnings outcome, this set of results puts the focus squarely on how durable the current profitability profile really is.

See our full analysis for IG Port.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the widely followed growth focused narratives around IG Port and where those stories might need adjusting.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Margins Slide From 7.6% To 4.7%

  • The trailing net profit margin sits at 4.7%, compared with 7.6% a year earlier, alongside trailing net income of ¥612 million on ¥13,119 million of revenue.
  • What stands out for a bullish view that focuses on long term growth is that a 22.2% five year annual earnings growth rate and forecast 36.2% annual earnings growth sit next to this margin step down, so:
    • Supporters of the bullish case can point to revenue forecasted to grow 14.8% a year versus 4.7% for the broader JP market, while acknowledging that profitability per yen of sales has been tighter over the last year.
    • At the same time, critics of that bullish stance can highlight that negative earnings growth in the most recent trailing year and the fall from a 7.6% to 4.7% margin mean delivery against those higher forecasts has become more dependent on future execution than on recent history.
To see how these mixed margin signals fit into the broader growth story, read the full narrative that investors are debating right now. 📊 Read the full IG Port Consensus Narrative.

TTM EPS Slides From ¥56.66 To ¥31.27

  • Trailing twelve month basic EPS is ¥31.27, down from ¥56.66 a year earlier, even though trailing revenue over the same window is still above ¥13.0b.
  • Bears who focus on earnings stability will say this roughly 45% drop in trailing EPS and the range of quarterly EPS from ¥2.87 to ¥12.65 backs their concern about volatility, yet:
    • The presence of five year earnings growth running at 22.2% a year counters the idea that weaker trailing EPS alone defines the business, because it shows that over a longer stretch the company has built up earnings from a lower base.
    • Forecasts for earnings to grow at 36.2% a year add another wrinkle for that bearish view, since if that growth occurs off the current ¥31.27 trailing EPS, the longer term earnings level implied is much higher than where the recent dip might suggest on its own.

High 45.8x P/E Versus 59% DCF Discount

  • The shares trade on a 45.8x P/E against a current price of ¥1,388, while the supplied DCF fair value of ¥3,408.31 implies the stock sits about 59.3% below that modelled value.
  • Supporters of a bullish stance tend to lean on that gap between the P/E and DCF signals as a sign the market is cautious, because:
    • The elevated 45.8x P/E compared with 42.6x for peers and 19.2x for the JP Entertainment industry suggests investors already pay a premium, yet the 59.3% discount to DCF fair value implies the current premium multiple is still well below what that model sees as justified.
    • When that valuation picture is set alongside forecast revenue growth of 14.8% a year and earnings growth of 36.2% a year versus the broader JP market, the bullish argument is that the stock may be priced like a high expectation name, but not yet like one that fully reflects those growth forecasts.

data.title: Shift (TSE:3697) Q1 EPS Softness Tests Bullish Growth And High P/E Narratives
data.description:

The company has seen quarterly revenue move from ¥30.8b in Q1 2025 to ¥34.8b in Q1 2026. Over the same quarters, basic EPS went from ¥8.53 to ¥6.82, against a trailing twelve month EPS of ¥33.26 on revenue of ¥134.5b. Taken together with trailing net income of ¥8.75b, the latest results point to a business where earnings power and margins are front of mind for investors trying to judge the consistency of performance.

Margins Drift as Profit Trails Revenue

  • Net income for Q1 2026 was ¥1,795m on revenue of ¥34,845m, compared with ¥2,653m on ¥34,407m in Q4 2025, so profit did not track the small step up in sales.
  • What stands out to bullish investors is that trailing 12 month net profit margin is 6.5% versus 5.2% the prior year, yet this latest quarter shows profit of ¥1,795m sitting below the recent Q4 figure of ¥2,653m, which
    • supports the bullish argument that the wider 12 month picture still reflects healthier profitability, even if a single quarter comes in softer on the bottom line,
    • but also reminds you that quarterly swings around that 6.5% margin level can still appear when comparing periods like Q1 2026 to Q4 2025.

  • Over the last 12 months, earnings grew 45.7% year over year and five year earnings growth averages 23.4% per year. This frames Q1 2026 EPS of ¥6.82 within a much stronger longer term trend.
  • Supporters of the bullish view lean heavily on that 45.7% trailing earnings growth and 23.4% five year average. When you line that up with trailing 12 month EPS of ¥33.26 and trailing revenue of ¥134,490m you get a picture that
    • backs the idea of a business that has produced higher profit over time relative to its earlier base, even though individual quarters like Q1 2026 and Q3 2025 both sit around the ¥6.8 EPS level,

data.title: Iluka Resources Limited's (ASX:ILU) Price Is Right But Growth Is Lacking After Shares Rocket 26%
data.description:
Those holding Iluka Resources Limited (ASX:ILU) shares would be relieved that the share price has rebounded 26% in the last thirty days, but it needs to keep going to repair the recent damage it has caused to investor portfolios. The last 30 days bring the annual gain to a very sharp 28%.

Even after such a large jump in price, Iluka Resources may still be sending bullish signals at the moment with its price-to-earnings (or "P/E") ratio of 15.4x, since almost half of all companies in Australia have P/E ratios greater than 22x and even P/E's higher than 41x are not unusual. However, the P/E might be low for a reason and it requires further investigation to determine if it's justified.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

While the market has experienced earnings growth lately, Iluka Resources' earnings have gone into reverse gear, which is not great. The P/E is probably low because investors think this poor earnings performance isn't going to get any better. If this is the case, then existing shareholders will probably struggle to get excited about the future direction of the share price.

See our latest analysis for Iluka Resources

Want the full picture on analyst estimates for the company? Then our free report on Iluka Resources will help you uncover what's on the horizon.
Advertisement

Is There Any Growth For Iluka Resources?

The only time you'd be truly comfortable seeing a P/E as low as Iluka Resources' is when the company's growth is on track to lag the market.

Taking a look back first, the company's earnings per share growth last year wasn't something to get excited about as it posted a disappointing decline of 30%. This means it has also seen a slide in earnings over the longer-term as EPS is down 62% in total over the last three years. So unfortunately, we have to acknowledge that the company has not done a great job of growing earnings over that time.

Shifting to the future, estimates from the seven analysts covering the company suggest earnings growth is heading into negative territory, declining 6.8% each year over the next three years. Meanwhile, the broader market is forecast to expand by 17% per annum, which paints a poor picture.

With this information, we are not surprised that Iluka Resources is trading at a P/E lower than the market. However, shrinking earnings are unlikely to lead to a stable P/E over the longer term. There's potential for the P/E to fall to even lower levels if the company doesn't improve its profitability.

The Final Word

Iluka Resources' stock might have been given a solid boost, but its P/E certainly hasn't reached any great heights. Generally, our preference is to limit the use of the price-to-earnings ratio to establishing what the market thinks about the overall health of a company.

As we suspected, our examination of Iluka Resources' analyst forecasts revealed that its outlook for shrinking earnings is contributing to its low P/E. Right now shareholders are accepting the low P/E as they concede future earnings probably won't provide any pleasant surprises. It's hard to see the share price rising strongly in the near future under these circumstances.

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, basic EPS is ¥118.37. This compares with quarterly EPS figures between about ¥26.86 and ¥35.61 over the past five quarters, so the rolling year still reflects stronger profit per share than the most recent single quarter.

  • For a bullish take, the combination of lower P/E and a price below that DCF fair value is often read as pricing in more caution than the recent 19.2% earnings growth and 5.4% margin would suggest. This can be seen as an opportunity if you think the current 12 month performance of ¥2,725 million net income on ¥50,822 million revenue is sustainable.

    • On the other hand, the fact that the share price still trades below the DCF fair value even after that earnings growth hints that some investors may be weighing the cash flow and balance sheet risks more heavily than the income statement alone.

Dividend yield versus cash flow and debt coverage

  • The dividend yield sits at 3.63%, but that payout is flagged as not well covered by free cash flow, and debt is also described as not well covered by operating cash flow. Both points speak directly to how much financial flexibility the company has behind the ¥2,725 million of net income it produced over the last 12 months.
  • For a more cautious, bearish style view, these cash flow points matter because they show that even with 19.2% earnings growth and a 5.4% net margin, the company still has to work harder on converting accounting profit into cash. That can limit how comfortable investors feel about that 3.63% dividend and the current level of debt.
    • Critics might stress that a dividend not well covered by free cash flow leaves less room to handle periods like Q4 2025, when Rent reported a net loss of ¥17 million on ¥11,776 million of revenue, without leaning on the balance sheet.
    • The comment that debt is not well covered by operating cash flow also sits uneasily alongside the valuation argument, since weaker cash coverage can help explain why a stock on 8.1x P/E and below the DCF fair value might not be getting a higher multiple right now.
